name: Predictive Ledger — Multi-tenant Aito Accounting Demo
description: Interactive workbook for the Predictive Ledger reference: 255 customers, 128K invoices in one shared Aito instance, with predictions scoped per-tenant via customer_id. Companion to the source code at github.com/AitoDotAI/aito-accounting-demo.

widgets.title: Predictive Ledger

config.content:
# Predictive Ledger — multi-tenant accounting on Aito

This workbook demonstrates how Aito powers a SaaS accounting product: **255 customer companies, 128 000 invoices, one shared Aito instance**, with predictions scoped per-tenant via `customer_id` in the `where` clause.

**Repo:** [github.com/AitoDotAI/aito-accounting-demo](https://github.com/AitoDotAI/aito-accounting-demo) · **Companion:** [aito-demo](https://github.com/AitoDotAI/aito-demo) (the e-commerce reference).

## Dataset

| Layer | Records |
|-------|---------|
| `customers` | 255 (geometric: 3 enterprise / 12 large / 48 midmarket / 192 small) |
| `invoices` | 128 000 (24-month spread, Zipfian vendor distribution) |
| `bank_transactions` | ~68 000 (linked to invoices via schema link) |
| `corporate_entities` | 1 362 real Finnish vendors from PRH YTJ |
| `overrides` | ~7 500 human corrections |
| `help_articles` + `help_impressions` | 120 + 14 500 (CTR-ranked drawer) |

## Aito operators used

- **`_predict`** — GL code, approver, vendor matching, anomaly scoring
- **`_relate`** — rule mining, override pattern discovery, chained sub-pattern drill
- **`_recommend`** — help article CTR ranking, "users who read this also read"
- **`_evaluate`** — held-out accuracy with per-case results

## The headline

Same vendor, different customer → different prediction, because the conditional probability is computed only over rows that match the where filter. **No per-tenant model.**

widgets.title: Instance Activity
Sample invoices (first 10)
Customers by tier
GL codes for one customer (CUST-0000)
Routing breakdown (rule / aito / human / none) for CUST-0000

sections.title: Multi-tenancy in one query
sections.description: Same vendor, different customers → different predictions. The where clause is the entire isolation mechanism.

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## Single-table multi-tenancy

Every query carries `customer_id` in `where`. Aito treats this as a conditional-probability filter — adding `customer_id` reduces the row set the prediction is computed over.

The queries below predict the GL code for **the same vendor** (Kardex Finland Oy) under different customers' contexts. Watch the predictions — they differ, because each customer's history routes that vendor differently.

widgets.title: Kardex Finland Oy — under CUST-0000 (16 000 invoices, enterprise)
Kardex Finland Oy — under CUST-0003 (4 000 invoices, large)
Kardex Finland Oy — under CUST-0100 (250 invoices, small/cold-start)

sections.title: GL Code Prediction
sections.description: Predict which GL account an invoice should be assigned to — with text-token highlights from the description field

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## GL Code Prediction

Aito predicts the GL code from any combination of invoice fields. The `description` is a Text field — Aito's analyzer tokenizes it, so individual words contribute lift independently.

With `select: [{$why: {highlight: {posPreTag: "<mark>", posPostTag: "</mark>"}}}]`, the response shows which exact tokens drove the match — that's how the demo's prediction tab paints `<mark>Bakery</mark> <mark>products</mark>` over the description text.

widgets.title: Predict GL — vendor + amount + category + description
Cold-start: small customer, novel vendor (low confidence = honest)
GL code accuracy on CUST-0000 (50-sample test set)

sections.title: Approver Routing
sections.description: Predict which approver should handle an invoice. The fixture generator ties approvers to (category, amount) so the signal is learnable.

widgets.title: Predict approver for a Kardex Finland Oy invoice (CUST-0000)
Same vendor, big-ticket amount → escalates to a senior signer
Approver accuracy on CUST-0000 (50-sample test set)

sections.title: Payment Matching
sections.description: Predict the linked invoice from a bank transaction's description + amount

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## Payment Matching

The `bank_transactions` table has an `invoice_id` column linked to `invoices.invoice_id` (a foreign key in Aito's index). `_predict invoice_id` returns the matching invoice — with the linked invoice's vendor + amount in the same response, no second query.

Description is a Text field, so the analyzer tokenizes it. "OP /VENDOR/ KARDEX FINLAND" matches "Kardex Finland Oy" via shared tokens.

widgets.title: Predict invoice_id from bank description + amount
Predict vendor_name (alternative matching field)
Garbage description → low confidence (honest uncertainty)

sections.title: Rule Mining
sections.description: Discover deterministic patterns with _relate, then chain to find compound sub-rules

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## Rule Mining

`_relate` finds statistical relationships between fields. For each (customer, condition value), it shows how strongly that condition predicts a target — with **exact support ratios** like 4123/4156, not ML estimates.

## Chained drill (poor-man's pattern proposition)

Aito's `_relate` takes a single conjunction in the where clause. To discover compound rules — `vendor=X & gl_code=Y → cost_centre=Z` — chain the calls: fix the discovered conjunction in `where`, run `_relate` against the next field. The third query below is exactly that pattern.

widgets.title: Top-level: which GL does category=telecom predict (CUST-0000)?
Drill: under telecom & gl_code=6200, who tends to approve?
Override patterns: which vendor's overrides cluster on a corrected GL?

sections.title: Anomaly Detection
sections.description: Inverse prediction: low-probability ground truth = anomaly signal

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## Anomaly Detection

Same `_predict` engine, used in scoring mode. For each posted invoice, ask Aito which GL it would predict — if the actual GL has a low `$p`, the invoice doesn't fit the customer's normal pattern.

**No separate anomaly model.** The two queries below show the same operator on a typical row vs an outlier.

widgets.title: Normal: known vendor + matching category (high $p)
Anomalous: known vendor with mismatched category (low $p on top)

sections.title: Help Recommendations
sections.description: _recommend over impressions: rank help articles by historical click-through-rate, then chain via prev_article_id for "users who read this also read"

widgets.title: How it works

config.content:
## CTR-ranked help

The demo's `?` drawer ranks help articles by goal-oriented `_recommend`: given a `(customer, page, query)` context, return the article most likely to lead to `goal: {clicked: true}`.

The second query chains via `prev_article_id` — the article the user clicked into the current view. Result: "users who read this also read" with no separate session API.

widgets.title: Top help articles for /invoices on CUST-0000
Users who read ART-INVOICES-101 also read…
